sections.title: 합니다 Style (격식체 높임)
sections.explanation: Korean has two main polite speech levels: 해요체 (informal polite) and 합니다체 (formal polite). 합니다체 is used in formal situations — news broadcasts, business presentations, military, public speeches, and with strangers you want to show maximum respect to. For action verbs: stem + -ㅂ니다 (vowel) or -습니다 (consonant). 가다 → 갑니다, 먹다 → 먹습니다, 하다 → 합니다. For questions: stem + -ㅂ니까? (vowel) or -습니까? (consonant). 가다 → 갑니까?, 먹다 → 먹습니까? For descriptive verbs: same endings. 좋다 → 좋습니다, 크다 → 큽니다. Past tense: -았/었습니다, -았/었습니까? 갔습니다, 먹었습니까? Negative: 안 갑니다, 못 먹습니다, 가지 않습니다.
sections.tip: 합니다체 is always safe when unsure about formality — it is never rude. In job interviews, first meetings with elders, and public speaking, default to 합니다체. Switch to 해요체 only when the other person signals it is okay.

examples.en: Where did you come from? (formal question)

exercises.prompt: What is the 합니다 form of 읽다 (to read)?
exercises.explanation: 읽다 has stem 읽 ending in a consonant, so add -습니다: 읽습니다.

exercises.prompt: How do you form a formal question from 알다 (to know)?
exercises.explanation: 알다 is a ㄹ verb. Before ㅂ, the ㄹ drops: 알 → 아 + ㅂ니까 = 압니까?

exercises.prompt: In which situation would 합니다체 be LEAST appropriate?
exercises.explanation: 합니다체 would be overly formal and stiff when texting a close friend. Friends typically use 반말 (informal) or 해요체 (informal polite). Job interviews and news broadcasts call for 합니다체.

sections.title: Written/Literary Style (문어체)
sections.explanation: Written Korean — found in newspapers, academic papers, books, and formal documents — uses the plain style (해라체) with specific endings. Statements: -다 for all verbs (간다, 먹는다, 좋다, 컸다). Questions: -(으)ㄴ가? or -는가? (좋은가?, 가는가?). Commands: -아/어라 (가라, 먹어라). Propositions: -자 (가자). In news and academic writing, the -다 ending is standard: 대통령이 발표했다 (The president announced). 경제가 성장한다 (The economy is growing). Note the present tense forms: action verbs use -ㄴ다/-는다 (not just -다). 가다 → 간다, 먹다 → 먹는다. Descriptive verbs use just -다: 좋다, 크다. This style is impersonal and objective — perfect for factual reporting.
sections.tip: Written style uses the same forms as indirect speech quotations (-ㄴ/는다, -다). If you master indirect speech quoting patterns, you already know how to write in literary Korean style.

examples.en: He quietly left the room (novel style)

exercises.prompt: How would a newspaper write '경제가 성장하다' (the economy grows) in present tense?
exercises.explanation: In written/literary style, action verbs in present tense use -ㄴ다/-는다. 성장하다 → 성장한다 (stem 하 ends in vowel, add -ㄴ다).

exercises.prompt: Which style would you use in an academic paper?
exercises.explanation: Academic papers in Korean use the plain written style (해라체/문어체) with -다 endings. 합니다체 is too speech-oriented, and 해요체 is too casual for academic writing.

exercises.prompt: What is the written-style present tense of 먹다 (to eat)?
exercises.explanation: Action verbs with consonant-ending stems use -는다 in written style: 먹 + 는다 = 먹는다.

sections.title: Honorific Vocabulary and -(으)시-
sections.explanation: Beyond speech levels, Korean has an honorific system that elevates the subject of the sentence. The suffix -(으)시- is inserted into the verb to honor the subject: 가다 → 가시다 (to go, honorific), 먹다 → 드시다 (special honorific word), 있다 → 계시다 (special honorific). In formal speech: 가십니다, 드십니까? Many verbs have entirely different honorific counterparts: 먹다/마시다 → 드시다 (eat/drink), 자다 → 주무시다 (sleep), 있다 → 계시다 (exist/be), 말하다 → 말씀하시다 (speak), 죽다 → 돌아가시다 (die/pass away), 주다 → 드리다 (give to someone respected). The particle 은/는 becomes 께서는 for honored subjects, and 에게 becomes 께. Example: 할머니께서 주무시고 계십니다 (Grandmother is sleeping — triple honorific: 께서 + 주무시다 + 계시다).
sections.tip: Always use honorifics when talking about someone older or of higher status, even when talking TO a friend. '우리 할머니가 잔다' is rude even in casual speech — say '우리 할머니께서 주무신다' because the honored subject requires it regardless of listener.

examples.en: Grandfather ate his meal (드시다 = honorific of 먹다)
Mother is sleeping (주무시다 = honorific of 자다)
The CEO spoke (말씀하시다 = honorific of 말하다)

exercises.prompt: What is the honorific form of 먹다 (to eat)?
exercises.explanation: 먹다 has a special honorific replacement word: 드시다. While 먹으시다 is sometimes heard, 드시다 is the proper honorific form.

exercises.prompt: '할머니께서 집에 계세요' uses which honorific form?
exercises.explanation: 계시다 is the special honorific form of 있다 (to exist/be). 할머니께서 집에 계세요 = Grandmother is at home (said respectfully).

exercises.prompt: Why should you say '교수님이 말씀하셨어요' instead of '교수님이 말했어요'?
options: The professor is the subject and deserves honorific language
exercises.explanation: When the subject is someone deserving respect (professor, elder, boss), you must use honorific vocabulary. 말하다 → 말씀하시다 for honored subjects.
